questions.questionText: A patient presents with low mood, anhedonia, decreased energy, weight loss, insomnia with early morning awakening, and diurnal variation (worse in morning). Minimum duration for diagnosis of major depressive episode is:
questions.optionB: 2 weeks (at least 5 symptoms including depressed mood or anhedonia for ≥2 weeks, nearly every day, causing functional impairment)
questions.explanation: Core concept: MDE diagnosis requires ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks. Why correct: DSM-5/ICD-11: at least 5 of 9 symptoms present for ≥2 weeks. Must include either (1) depressed mood or (2) anhedonia. Other symptoms: weight change, sleep disturbance, psychomotor agitation/retardation, fatigue, worthlessness/guilt, concentration difficulty, suicidal ideation. Why A wrong: 1 week — insufficient duration. Why C wrong: 6 months — not required (this is for dysthymia/persistent depressive disorder = ≥2 years). Why D wrong: 1 month — not the criteria. Clinical relevance: Early morning awakening + diurnal variation (worse AM) = biological/melancholic depression (good response to antidepressants). SSRIs are first-line (fluoxetine, sertraline, escitalopram). Exam trap: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) = ≥2 years; MDE = ≥2 weeks. Duration is a critical differentiator. Memory aid: '2 weeks + 5 symptoms (including Sad or not eNJOYing) = MDE.' Reference: Ahuja – Mood Disorders.

questions.questionText: A patient with bipolar disorder develops lithium toxicity. Which triad of symptoms is characteristic?
questions.optionB: Tremor, diarrhea/vomiting, and neurological symptoms (ataxia, dysarthria, coarse tremor — lithium toxicity: coarse tremor, cerebellar signs, GI symptoms)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Lithium toxicity = coarse tremor + GI symptoms + neurological dysfunction. Why correct: Lithium narrow therapeutic index (0.6-1.2 mEq/L). Toxicity: GI (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea), neurological (coarse tremor replacing fine tremor, ataxia, dysarthria, confusion, seizures), cardiac (arrhythmias). Why A wrong: Fever + rigidity + altered consciousness = NMS (neuroleptic malignant syndrome — antipsychotic complication). Why C wrong: Liver failure triad. Why D wrong: Anticholinergic side effects (atypical antipsychotics, TCAs). Clinical relevance: Lithium side effects at therapeutic levels: fine tremor, polyuria (nephrogenic DI), hypothyroidism, weight gain, renal impairment. Monitoring: lithium levels every 3-6 months, TFT, renal function. Precipitants of toxicity: dehydration, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides. Exam trap: Fine tremor = therapeutic side effect; COARSE tremor = toxicity sign. This distinction is key. Memory aid: 'Lithium toxicity = Lots of shaking (coarse) + Loose stools + Loss of coordination.' Reference: Ahuja – Mood Stabilizers.
questions.conceptTag: Lithium toxicity coarse tremor ataxia GI narrow

questions.questionText: First-line pharmacotherapy for bipolar disorder (acute mania) is:
questions.optionB: Lithium (gold standard mood stabilizer — prevents both manic and depressive episodes; also valproate, carbamazepine for acute mania)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Lithium is the gold standard for bipolar disorder. Why correct: Lithium: effective for acute mania + maintenance (prevents relapse of both mania and depression). Also reduces suicide risk (unique benefit). Alternatives: valproate (rapid cycling, mixed episodes), carbamazepine, atypical antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine). Why A wrong: SSRIs — used for depression but can precipitate mania in bipolar patients (must use with mood stabilizer). Why C wrong: Benzodiazepines — adjunctive for agitation/insomnia, not primary treatment. Why D wrong: TCAs — even more likely to trigger mania than SSRIs in bipolar. Clinical relevance: Valproate contraindicated in women of childbearing age (teratogenic — neural tube defects). Lithium: Ebstein's anomaly risk (much lower than previously thought). Monitoring: lithium levels, TFT, creatinine. Exam trap: NEVER give antidepressants alone in bipolar — always with mood stabilizer (risk of manic switch). Memory aid: 'Lithium = Legendary mood stabilizer (gold standard for bipolar).' Reference: Ahuja – Pharmacotherapy.
questions.conceptTag: Lithium bipolar mania gold standard mood stabilizer
